title: 289-Million-Year-Old Mummy Reptile Reveals How We Started Breathing
summary:
Every time you take a breath, you're using a system that's nearly 300 million years old!

Scientists found a tiny, mummified reptile in a cave in Oklahoma that's 289 million years old. It's called Captorhinus aguti, and it's only a few inches long. But this little fossil is packed with amazing details—it still has its skin, cartilage (the stuff in your ears and nose), and even traces of ancient proteins!

Using special scanners, researchers looked inside the fossil without breaking it. They saw something incredible: the reptile had a rib-based breathing system, just like the one you use right now. Muscles between its ribs pulled its chest out, letting air rush into its lungs.

Before this system evolved, early land animals breathed through their skin and used their mouths to push air into their lungs—like a frog. That works, but it's not very efficient. Rib breathing is way better because it brings in more oxygen and lets animals run around without getting tired so fast.

"It was a game changer that allowed these animals to adopt a much more active lifestyle," said researcher Ethan Mooney.

And here's another wow: the proteins found in this fossil are nearly 100 million years older than any ever found before. That's like finding a message in a bottle from almost the beginning of life on land!
why_it_matters: Every time you take a deep breath while playing, you're using a system that started with small reptiles like this one — it connects you to ancient life.

keywords.explanation: A tiny, lizard-like reptile that lived 289 million years ago.
The preserved remains of an ancient animal, found in a cave in Oklahoma.
A way of breathing using muscles between the ribs to pull air into the lungs.
The outer covering of the reptile, which was still preserved in the fossil.
A flexible tissue, like in your ears and nose, also found in the fossil.
Molecules that make up living things; the fossil had very old proteins.

questions.question: How did scientists look inside the fossil without breaking it?

background_read: Before animals could live on land, they needed a way to breathe air. Early animals used their skin or mouth to get oxygen, but that was slow.
Rib breathing is the system you use right now. It lets you take big breaths so you can run and play without getting tired quickly.
Oklahoma's Richards Spur fossil site is famous for preserving soft tissues like skin because oil and mud protected the remains from decay.
Article_Structure: WHO: Researchers studying a tiny fossil reptile called Captorhinus aguti
WHAT: They found it had rib-based breathing, the same system humans use today
WHERE: In a cave in Oklahoma, at the Richards Spur site
WHY: This shows how breathing evolved nearly 300 million years ago and helped animals become active

perspectives.perspective: Scientists are amazed by the preservation
perspectives.description: They can't believe the fossil still has skin and proteins after so many millions of years.
Learning that we share a breathing system with an ancient reptile makes science come alive.
If scientists used old methods, they might have broken the fossil, but the new scanner kept it safe.
